skills.prompt: This topic covers classifying triangles for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.5(A)). Students should understand two ways to classify triangles: by sides and by angles. Key concepts: by sides - equilateral (all sides equal), isosceles (exactly two sides equal), scalene (no sides equal); by angles - acute (all angles less than 90 degrees), right (one angle equals 90 degrees), obtuse (one angle greater than 90 degrees). Students should classify triangles using both criteria simultaneously (e.g., 'right isosceles triangle'). The sum of interior angles of any triangle is 180 degrees. Vocabulary: triangle, equilateral, isosceles, scalene, acute, right, obtuse, classify, sides, angles, vertex, congruent. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

This topic covers classifying triangles for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.5(A)). Students should understand two ways to classify triangles: by sides and by angles. Key concepts: by sides - equilateral (all sides equal), isosceles (exactly two sides equal), scalene (no sides equal); by angles - acute (all angles less than 90 degrees), right (one angle equals 90 degrees), obtuse (one angle greater than 90 degrees). Students should classify triangles using both criteria simultaneously (e.g., 'right isosceles triangle'). The sum of interior angles of any triangle is 180 degrees. Vocabulary: triangle, equilateral, isosceles, scalene, acute, right, obtuse, classify, sides, angles, vertex, congruent. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

This topic covers classifying triangles for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.5(A)). Students should understand two ways to classify triangles: by sides and by angles. Key concepts: by sides - equilateral (all sides equal), isosceles (exactly two sides equal), scalene (no sides equal); by angles - acute (all angles less than 90 degrees), right (one angle equals 90 degrees), obtuse (one angle greater than 90 degrees). Students should classify triangles using both criteria simultaneously (e.g., 'right isosceles triangle'). The sum of interior angles of any triangle is 180 degrees. Vocabulary: triangle, equilateral, isosceles, scalene, acute, right, obtuse, classify, sides, angles, vertex, congruent. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

This topic covers classifying and identifying quadrilaterals for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.5(A)). Students should understand the properties and relationships among different types of quadrilaterals. Key concepts: a quadrilateral has four sides and four angles; parallelograms have two pairs of parallel sides; rectangles are parallelograms with four right angles; rhombuses are parallelograms with four equal sides; squares are both rectangles and rhombuses; trapezoids have exactly one pair of parallel sides. Students should understand the hierarchy of quadrilateral relationships (a square is always a rectangle, but a rectangle is not always a square). Vocabulary: quadrilateral, parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, square, trapezoid, parallel, perpendicular, right angle, side, congruent, classify. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

This topic covers properties of polygons for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.5(A)). Students should understand the characteristics that define different types of polygons. Key concepts: a polygon is a closed figure made of straight line segments; polygons are named by their number of sides (triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, hexagon, etc.); regular polygons have all sides and all angles equal; irregular polygons do not have all sides and angles equal; students should classify polygons by number of sides and regularity. Vocabulary: polygon, regular, irregular, side, angle, vertex, triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, hexagon, heptagon, octagon, closed figure, classify. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

skills.skill: Volume of irregular figures made of unit cubes
skills.prompt: This topic covers volume of irregular figures made of unit cubes for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.6(A)). Students should understand how to determine the volume of irregular 3D figures by counting unit cubes. Key concepts: volume measures the amount of space a 3D figure occupies; for irregular figures, count each unit cube including hidden cubes; volume is expressed in cubic units; students can use systematic counting strategies (layer by layer) to avoid missing cubes; irregular figures cannot use a simple length x width x height formula. Vocabulary: volume, unit cube, cubic units, irregular, 3D figure, count, layer, space, three-dimensional, compose, decompose. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

skills.skill: Volume of rectangular prisms made of unit cubes
skills.prompt: This topic covers volume of rectangular prisms made of unit cubes for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.6(A)). Students should understand that volume can be found by counting unit cubes or by using the relationship between counting and multiplication. Key concepts: volume is the number of unit cubes that fill a 3D figure; a rectangular prism's volume equals length x width x height (all in the same units); students can count cubes in layers and multiply by the number of layers; volume is measured in cubic units (cubic centimeters, cubic inches, etc.). Students should connect the counting method to the formula. Vocabulary: volume, unit cube, rectangular prism, length, width, height, layer, cubic units, three-dimensional, fill, formula. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

skills.skill: Compare and convert customary units of length
skills.prompt: This topic covers converting between measurement units for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.7(A)). Students should understand how to convert between units within the same measurement system. Key concepts: customary units include inches/feet/yards/miles (length), ounces/pounds/tons (weight), and cups/pints/quarts/gallons (capacity); metric units include millimeters/centimeters/meters/kilometers (length), grams/kilograms (mass), and milliliters/liters (volume); converting to smaller units requires multiplication; converting to larger units requires division; metric conversions are based on powers of 10. Vocabulary: convert, customary, metric, unit, length, weight, mass, capacity, volume, multiply, divide, equivalent, conversion factor. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

This topic covers coordinate planes and graphing points for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.8(A)). Students should understand how to use a coordinate system to locate and plot points. Key concepts: the coordinate plane has a horizontal x-axis and vertical y-axis that intersect at the origin (0,0); points are located using ordered pairs (x, y); the x-coordinate tells how far to move left/right, the y-coordinate tells how far to move up/down; in Grade 5, students may work with the first quadrant or all four quadrants. Students should plot points accurately and identify coordinates of given points. Vocabulary: coordinate plane, x-axis, y-axis, origin, ordered pair, quadrant, plot, point, horizontal, vertical, coordinate. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

Vocabulary: coordinate plane, x-axis, y-axis, origin, ordered pair, quadrant, plot, point, horizontal, vertical, coordinate. Problems should be appropriate for 10-11 year olds.

This topic covers Graph a two-variable relationship for Grade 5 students (TEKS 5.8(C)) in the area of geometry and measurement. Students should understand the key geometric concepts, properties, and measurement techniques related to this topic. Key skills include identifying and classifying shapes, understanding spatial relationships, measuring attributes, applying formulas, and solving real-world problems involving geometry. Vocabulary related to geometric shapes, angles, measurement, and spatial reasoning should be explicitly taught. Problems should involve both visual identification and computation, appropriate for 10-11 year olds and aligned with Texas TEKS standards.

Problems should involve both visual identification and computation, appropriate for 10-11 year olds and aligned with Texas TEKS standards.
